sources: Standard Methods for Thermal Comfort Assessment of Clothing
Effect of Mechanical and Physical Properties on Fabric Hand
Fabric for Fashion: The Complete Guide
Knitting Science, Technology, Process and Material
Human Body: A Wearable Product Designers Guide
Designing Apparel for Consumers: Body Shape and Size
Materials and Technology for Sportswear and Performance Apparel
Textiles and Fashion: Materials, Design and Technology
Textile Calculation: Fibre to Finished Garment (2023)
Assessing the Environmental Impact of Textiles and the Clothing Supply Chain
Principles of Colour and Appearance Measurement (Roy Choudhury)
Pattern Cutting: The Architecture of Fashion
Waterproof and Water Repellent Textiles and Clothing
Claude Opus 4.6 trained knowledge (textile science, polymer chemistry, fiber morphology)
changelog_v2_to_v3: Added 15 new fiber entries (35 total, up from 20)
Added 18 new construction entries (48 total, up from 30)
Added 8 new silhouette effect rules (14 total, up from 6)
Added 12 new blend rules (30 total, up from 18)
Added NEW section: dyeingSystems (12 dye classes + 8 print methods)
Added NEW section: sustainabilityMetrics (LCA data per fiber)
Added NEW section: patternEngineering (grain rules, seam allowances, marker efficiency)
Added NEW section: colorScience (CIE L*a*b*, Delta-E, metamerism)
Added NEW section: defectTaxonomy (fabric + garment defects)
Added NEW section: trims (zippers, buttons, elastics, interfacing types)
Deepened all existing fiber entries with chemical resistance, UV resistance, biodegradability
Added KES-F and FAST system references per construction

morphology.cross_section: kidney bean / collapsed tube with lumen
morphology.longitudinal: flat twisted ribbon with convolutions (65-80 per cm)

friction.wet_behavior: increases significantly — fiber swells, surface roughness rises

thermal.insulation_note: moderate insulator, poor when wet — loses ~50% insulation

moisture.wicking: moderate — absorbs moisture into fiber structure (hydrophilic cellulose)

physics.uv_resistance: moderate — degrades with prolonged exposure, yellowing
physics.pilling_tendency: low (short fibers shed rather than pill)

chemical.acids: harmed by mineral acids, tolerates dilute organic acids
chemical.bleach: tolerant (sodium hypochlorite), also tolerates hydrogen peroxide
chemical.biological: susceptible to mildew, bacteria, rot in prolonged damp conditions

visual.surface: matte with subtle natural texture, visible fiber character
visual.foldCharacter: soft rounded folds in lightweight; angular creases in heavy canvas
visual.lightBehavior: diffuse matte, absorbs light, no sheen or shine
visual.transparency: opaque in standard weights, semi-transparent below 100 GSM
visual.wrinklePattern: visible creasing, holds wrinkles, natural lived-in appearance
visual.movement: moves with body in knits, holds shape in woven
visual.colorUptake: excellent — accepts reactive, direct, vat, sulfur dyes readily

promptFragments.drape: soft natural drape, fabric moves with body
promptFragments.surface: smooth matte cotton surface with subtle knit/weave texture
promptFragments.folds: soft natural folds with gentle creasing
promptFragments.light: matte diffuse lighting, no specular highlights on fabric

skin_interaction.comfort_rating: excellent — hypoallergenic, most comfortable natural fiber
skin_interaction.thermal_feel: warm to touch initially, neutral at body temperature
skin_interaction.moisture_comfort: absorbs sweat but slow to dry — can feel clammy in heavy exercise

production.careInstructions: Machine wash cold to warm, tumble dry low to medium

sustainability.pesticide_share: 2.5% of land but 16% of world's insecticides

wool.name: Wool (Sheep)
wool.source: Ovis aries — primarily Merino, Rambouillet, Corriedale, Romney

friction.directional_property: different root-to-tip vs tip-to-root due to scales — DFE (Directional Frictional Effect) causes felting
friction.wet_behavior: dramatically increases — felting occurs under mechanical action + moisture + heat

thermal.heat_of_sorption: significant — releases heat when absorbing moisture (exothermic)

moisture.wicking: slow initial absorption, excellent moisture buffering
moisture.comfort_note: absorbs 30% of weight before feeling damp

physics.abrasion_resistance: moderate — pilling occurs in fine wools

chemical.composition: keratin protein (disulfide cross-links, peptide bonds, salt linkages, hydrogen bonds)
chemical.acids: resistant to dilute acids — used in carbonization to remove vegetable matter
chemical.alkalis: harmed — even mild alkali damages wool (pH >10 dissolves)
chemical.bleach: harmed by chlorine (destroys fiber); hydrogen peroxide OK for bleaching
chemical.solvents: resistant to most organic solvents; dry cleaning safe
chemical.biological: resistant to mildew, susceptible to moth larvae (keratin)

visual.surface: matte with texture from fiber crimp, surface haze from protruding fibers
visual.foldCharacter: soft rolling folds, springs back, no sharp creases
visual.lightBehavior: matte diffuse, absorbs light, soft shadow depth in folds
visual.wrinklePattern: resists wrinkling, wrinkles hang out over time
visual.movement: drapes with body and weight, bounces slightly, has substance
visual.colorUptake: excellent — accepts acid, chrome, reactive dyes; deep vivid colors

promptFragments.drape: structured drape with natural weight and body
promptFragments.surface: matte wool surface with visible fiber texture and slight surface haze
promptFragments.folds: soft rolling folds with gentle depth, fabric springs back
promptFragments.light: matte diffuse lighting, deep soft shadows in folds

skin_interaction.comfort_rating: excellent for fine merino (<19um), irritating for coarse (>25um)
skin_interaction.thermal_feel: warm to touch, excellent temperature regulation
skin_interaction.irritation_risk: fiber diameter dependent — itch threshold ~25 micrometers

production.careInstructions: Dry clean or hand wash cold. Superwash: machine wash gentle cold

friction.scroop: characteristic rustling sound in degummed silk from surface friction

thermal.insulation_note: moderate — warm in winter, cool in summer (excellent thermal regulation)

tensile.note: strongest natural fiber by weight — tensile strength comparable to steel wire of same diameter

physics.uv_resistance: poor — yellows and degrades rapidly in sunlight

chemical.composition: fibroin (75-83%) + sericin (17-25%) — sericin removed in degumming
chemical.acids: harmed by mineral acids, resistant to organic acids
chemical.bleach: harmed by chlorine; hydrogen peroxide bleaching only
chemical.biological: susceptible to carpet beetles, not moths

visual.surface: lustrous, smooth, light-catching with characteristic silk sheen
visual.foldCharacter: fine fluid folds that drape smoothly, no angular creasing
visual.lightBehavior: high luster from triangular cross-section, directional sheen
visual.transparency: semi-transparent in lightweight, opaque in heavier weights
visual.wrinklePattern: fine creases that smooth with hanging
visual.movement: fluid, liquid drape, follows body contours, catches light
visual.colorUptake: excellent — deep vibrant colors, accepts acid and reactive dyes

promptFragments.drape: fluid liquid drape that falls close to the body
promptFragments.surface: smooth lustrous silk surface with directional sheen
promptFragments.folds: fine fluid folds with elegant draping
promptFragments.light: specular highlights on fabric, directional sheen that shifts with movement

skin_interaction.comfort_rating: excellent — hypoallergenic, amino acid composition similar to human skin
skin_interaction.thermal_feel: cool to touch, excellent temperature regulation

commonFinishes: weighted (historic — adds body with metallic salts)
production.careInstructions: Dry clean recommended, or hand wash cold with mild detergent

sustainability.animal_welfare_concern: silkworm killed in cocoon harvesting (except peace silk/ahimsa)

thermal.insulation_note: highest thermal conductivity of natural fibers — feels cool

moisture.wicking: excellent — fastest wicking of natural fibers

tensile.note: strongest common natural fiber, very low elongation — stiff, does not stretch

chemical.biological: susceptible to mildew in damp conditions

visual.surface: natural texture with visible slubs and fiber nodes
visual.foldCharacter: sharp angular creases, wrinkles deeply — this IS the aesthetic
visual.lightBehavior: matte with subtle natural sheen that develops with washing
visual.movement: structured drape, holds away from body creating volume
visual.colorUptake: good — accepts same dye classes as cotton but slightly less even uptake

promptFragments.drape: structured drape with natural body, fabric holds its shape
promptFragments.surface: textured linen surface with visible slubs and natural irregularity
promptFragments.folds: crisp angular folds with characteristic linen creasing
promptFragments.light: matte finish with subtle natural sheen, no synthetic shine

skin_interaction.comfort_rating: excellent in warm weather — cool, moisture-wicking
skin_interaction.thermal_feel: noticeably cool to touch (high thermal conductivity)

physics.uv_resistance: good — better than nylon, degrades slowly
physics.pilling_tendency: moderate to high — pills anchor to strong fiber

chemical.composition: PET polymer via polycondensation of ethylene glycol + terephthalic acid
chemical.alkalis: degraded by strong alkali at high temp
chemical.solvents: resistant to most except some phenols

visual.surface: smooth uniform, matte to high sheen depending on fiber modification
visual.foldCharacter: rounded folds, resists sharp creases, springs back
visual.lightBehavior: trilobal = silk-like luster; round = matte; hollow = light diffusion
visual.colorUptake: requires disperse dyes at high temperature (130C) or carrier-assisted

promptFragments.drape: {varies by construction — can mimic silk or maintain structure}
promptFragments.surface: smooth uniform polyester surface with {matte/slight sheen}
promptFragments.folds: rounded resilient folds that spring back
promptFragments.light: {matte diffuse / subtle sheen depending on fiber variant}

tensile.note: exceptional abrasion resistance — 10x cotton

physics.uv_resistance: poor — degrades and yellows in sunlight (nylon 6,6 worse than nylon 6)
physics.abrasion_resistance: outstanding — highest of any common apparel fiber

visual.surface: smooth with subtle sheen
visual.foldCharacter: soft folds with good recovery, fluid for a synthetic
visual.lightBehavior: subtle to moderate luster
visual.movement: fluid and stretchy, drapes well in fine deniers

promptFragments.surface: smooth nylon surface with subtle sheen
promptFragments.folds: soft resilient folds with elastic recovery

viscose_rayon.name: Viscose Rayon
viscose_rayon.process: dissolved wood pulp (xanthation with carbon disulfide) extruded through spinneret

visual.surface: smooth with subtle sheen, slightly reflective
visual.foldCharacter: soft fluid folds, excellent drape, falls close to body
visual.lightBehavior: moderate sheen, between cotton matte and silk luster
visual.movement: fluid and flowing, silk-like drape at lower cost

promptFragments.drape: soft fluid drape that falls close to the body
promptFragments.surface: smooth viscose surface with subtle sheen
promptFragments.folds: soft flowing folds with elegant draping
promptFragments.light: moderate sheen, softer than silk but more lustrous than cotton

lyocell_tencel.name: Lyocell (Tencel)
lyocell_tencel.process: dissolved wood pulp in NMMO (N-Methylmorpholine N-oxide) solvent — closed-loop, 99.5% solvent recovery

moisture.wicking: excellent — superior to cotton and viscose

tensile.note: strongest regenerated cellulosic — key advantage over viscose

visual.surface: smooth with peach-skin softness, subtle luster
visual.foldCharacter: soft flowing folds, beautiful drape
visual.movement: fluid, silk-like movement with better body than viscose
visual.fibrillation_note: fibrillates when wet — can be used for peach-skin effect or must be controlled with crosslinking

promptFragments.surface: smooth peach-soft surface with subtle matte sheen
promptFragments.folds: soft flowing folds with elegant natural drape
promptFragments.light: subtle matte sheen, softer glow than silk

spandex_elastane.name: Spandex / Elastane (Lycra)
spandex_elastane.polymer: segmented polyurethane (hard crystalline + soft amorphous segments)

thermal.note: degrades with chlorine, high heat, UV

physics.abrasion_resistance: poor alone — always used in blend

blend_effects.at_2_5_percent: comfort stretch and shape retention for wovens
blend_effects.visual_effect: low%: minimal change; high%: smooth body-hugging surface

cashmere.name: Cashmere
cashmere.source: undercoat of Capra hircus goat (primarily Inner Mongolia, Outer Mongolia, China)

morphology.cross_section: oval, finer scales than wool (lower scale frequency)

thermal.insulation_note: 3x warmer than sheep wool by weight due to extremely fine fiber diameter creating more air pockets

moisture.wicking: moderate, similar to wool but finer structure

tensile.note: weaker than sheep wool — pills easily under abrasion

physics.pilling_tendency: high — inherent to fiber fineness, not a defect

visual.surface: ultra-soft matte surface with cloud-like texture, no visible fiber structure
visual.foldCharacter: soft rounded folds with exceptional loft, fabric has pillowy quality
visual.lightBehavior: deep matte, absorbs light, no sheen whatsoever
visual.wrinklePattern: good wrinkle recovery, smooths with hanging
visual.movement: light drape with body, floats rather than clings

promptFragments.drape: light floating drape with pillowy softness
promptFragments.surface: ultra-soft matte cashmere surface with cloud-like texture
promptFragments.folds: soft rounded folds with exceptional loft and depth
promptFragments.light: deep matte, fabric absorbs light creating soft shadows

skin_interaction.comfort_rating: exceptional — finest animal fiber commonly used
skin_interaction.irritation_risk: very low — fiber too fine to trigger itch response

production.pilling_note: pills easily — inherent characteristic. Anti-pilling treatments reduce but don't eliminate
production.careInstructions: Dry clean preferred. Hand wash cold in cashmere-specific detergent, lay flat to dry
production.adulteration_note: widespread — DNA testing reveals up to 30% of 'cashmere' contains wool or yak

sustainability.overgrazing_concern: severe — Mongolian grassland desertification linked to goat herd expansion

morphology.cross_section: nearly round, smooth scales (much flatter than wool — resists felting)

tensile.note: strong, resilient, excellent luster, resists felting due to smooth scales

physics.abrasion_resistance: good — resists pilling better than wool

visual.surface: distinctive luster — the most lustrous animal fiber, visible sheen even in dark colors
visual.foldCharacter: resilient soft folds with visible fiber halo
visual.lightBehavior: high luster, catches light beautifully, silk-like sheen
visual.movement: light and airy with visible fiber halo around fabric surface

promptFragments.surface: lustrous mohair surface with soft fiber halo and visible sheen
promptFragments.light: high natural luster, light catches fibers creating soft glow

sustainability.animal_welfare_concern: PETA campaigns led major brands to drop mohair — RMS certification addresses this

alpaca.name: Alpaca
alpaca.source: Vicugna pacos (huacaya breed = crimped/fluffy; suri breed = silky/dreadlocked)

thermal.insulation_note: warmer than wool — hollow fiber core traps air. 5x warmer than sheep wool by weight

moisture.note: lower regain than wool — stays drier

tensile.note: stronger than wool at equivalent diameter, no directional scales — hypoallergenic, no felting

visual.surface: soft matte with subtle luster, smoother than wool
visual.foldCharacter: soft drape with weight, luxurious hang
visual.lightBehavior: subtle natural sheen, less than mohair but more than wool
visual.movement: drapes with beautiful weight, structured yet soft

promptFragments.surface: soft matte alpaca surface with subtle natural luster
promptFragments.folds: soft weighted folds with luxurious drape
promptFragments.light: subtle natural sheen, matte with gentle highlights

production.natural_colors: 22 recognized natural shades — can be used undyed

thermal.conductivity_note: similar to linen — high conductivity, cool wearing

tensile.note: strongest natural textile fiber

physics.uv_resistance: excellent — better than cotton and linen

visual.surface: similar to linen but coarser texture, visible slub irregularity
visual.foldCharacter: stiff angular folds, softens significantly with washing
visual.lightBehavior: matte natural finish
visual.wrinklePattern: wrinkles like linen but more so

promptFragments.surface: natural textured hemp surface with visible fiber character
promptFragments.folds: angular organic folds with natural creasing
promptFragments.light: matte natural finish, no synthetic shine

moisture.antimicrobial: claimed but debated — most antimicrobial properties lost in viscose processing

tensile.note: similar to standard viscose — wet strength is a concern

visual.surface: smooth, soft, subtle sheen
visual.foldCharacter: fluid draping folds
visual.lightBehavior: subtle sheen similar to viscose

promptFragments.surface: smooth soft bamboo surface with subtle natural sheen
promptFragments.folds: soft fluid folds with gentle drape

production.note: FTC requires labeling as 'rayon made from bamboo' — chemically processed viscose, not mechanical bamboo fiber

sustainability.greenwashing_risk: high — marketed as eco but uses same viscose process with CS2

modal.name: Modal / MicroModal
modal.process: modified viscose process with higher DP and crystallinity — beech wood pulp

tensile.note: much better wet strength than viscose (50%)

visual.surface: exceptionally smooth, subtle pearlescent sheen
visual.foldCharacter: fluid soft drape, more body than viscose
visual.lightBehavior: subtle pearlescent quality, softer than silk sheen

promptFragments.surface: ultra-smooth modal surface with soft pearlescent quality
promptFragments.folds: fluid soft folds with elegant drape and body

cupro.name: Cupro (Bemberg)
cupro.process: cuprammonium process using cotton linter waste — dissolved in copper and ammonia solution

tensile.note: good strength, excellent recovery

visual.surface: silk-like luster, very smooth
visual.foldCharacter: fluid drape nearly identical to silk
visual.lightBehavior: high luster, silk-like sheen

promptFragments.surface: silk-like smooth cupro surface with elegant luster
promptFragments.folds: fluid silk-like folds with liquid drape
promptFragments.light: high luster, directional sheen like silk

acetate.name: Acetate
acetate.process: cellulose acetylation (acetic acid + acetic anhydride) — technically modified cellulose, not regenerated

tensile.note: weak fiber, poor abrasion resistance

chemical.critical_care_constraint: DISSOLVES in acetone, nail polish remover, certain perfumes

visual.surface: high luster, silk-like appearance
visual.foldCharacter: crisp drape with body
visual.lightBehavior: high sheen, catches light — used for its visual luxury at low cost

promptFragments.surface: lustrous acetate surface with silk-like sheen
promptFragments.light: high sheen, specular highlights, luxury appearance

production.primary_use: linings, formal wear where appearance > durability

morphology.cross_section: dog-bone or round depending on spinning method

thermal.insulation_note: excellent insulator — lightweight warmth, wool substitute

physics.uv_resistance: excellent — best of common apparel fibers
physics.pilling_tendency: moderate to high — strong anchor fiber

visual.surface: wool-like appearance but more uniform, can be very soft with brushing
visual.foldCharacter: soft rounded folds, similar to wool but lighter
visual.lightBehavior: matte to semi-matte, can mimic wool closely

promptFragments.surface: soft wool-like acrylic surface with uniform texture
promptFragments.folds: soft rounded folds with light body

production.primary_use: knitwear, sweaters, blankets — wool substitute at budget price

morphology.note: lightest textile fiber — floats on water

thermal.note: very heat sensitive — lowest safe pressing temp

visual.surface: waxy appearance, matte

production.primary_use: base layers, thermal underwear, activewear linings, non-woven (disposable)

morphology.note: one of the oldest textile fibers — 5000+ years

tensile.note: extremely strong — even stronger than hemp. Very low elongation

visual.surface: natural sheen, smoother than linen
visual.foldCharacter: stiff, angular, very crisp

production.note: often blended with cotton or linen to reduce stiffness

vicuna.name: Vicuña
vicuna.source: Vicugna vicugna — wild South American camelid, sheared every 2-3 years

diameter_micrometers.note: finest animal fiber in commercial use

thermal.insulation_note: warmest natural fiber by weight — interlocking air pockets from extreme fineness

tensile.note: delicate — must be handled with extreme care in processing

visual.surface: incredibly soft, ultra-fine, golden-cinnamon color (natural, rarely dyed)
visual.lightBehavior: subtle warm glow, matte
visual.movement: weightless drape, barely perceptible on skin

promptFragments.surface: weightless ultra-soft vicuña surface with natural golden-cinnamon tone

production.note: most expensive fiber in world — single vicuña coat can retail $20,000-50,000

qiviut.name: Qiviut (Musk Ox)
qiviut.source: Ovibos moschatus — Arctic musk ox undercoat, shed naturally or combed

thermal.insulation_note: 8x warmer than sheep wool by weight — evolved for -60C Arctic conditions

moisture.note: does not shrink when wet — no felting (smooth fiber surface)

tensile.note: soft but durable, does not felt

visual.surface: smoky brown natural color, incredibly soft matte
visual.lightBehavior: matte, absorbs light

camel_hair.name: Camel Hair
camel_hair.source: Bactrian camel (Camelus bactrianus) — inner down undercoat

thermal.insulation_note: excellent — hollow fiber medulla provides natural insulation, comparable to cashmere

tensile.note: moderate strength, good elasticity

visual.surface: natural tan/brown color — rarely dyed (natural color valued)
visual.foldCharacter: soft drape with body, similar to cashmere but heavier
visual.lightBehavior: warm matte with subtle luster

promptFragments.surface: warm natural tan camel hair surface with soft matte finish

thermal.insulation_note: comparable to cashmere — hollow fiber structure, warmer than merino

tensile.note: stronger than cashmere, less pilling

visual.surface: dark brown to black natural color, soft matte
visual.foldCharacter: similar to cashmere but slightly heavier

angora_rabbit.name: Angora (Rabbit)
angora_rabbit.source: Angora rabbit (Oryctolagus cuniculus) — NOT Angora goat (that's mohair)

thermal.insulation_note: extremely warm — hollow fiber core, 2.5x warmer than wool

tensile.note: very weak — always blended (typically 20-40% angora with wool or nylon)

physics.felting_tendency: extremely high — fiber is very slippery, sheds easily

visual.surface: distinctive fluffy halo, incredibly soft visible fiber cloud around fabric
visual.lightBehavior: diffuse light halo effect

production.animal_welfare_concern: significant — many brands banned angora after PETA investigations (2013)

morphology.note: second most produced natural fiber after cotton

tensile.note: stiff and brittle

chemical.composition: 65% cellulose, 22% hemicellulose, 13% lignin (lignin causes stiffness and yellowing)

visual.surface: coarse, natural golden-brown, rustic texture
visual.foldCharacter: very stiff, no drape

production.primary_use: sacks, bags, home furnishing, increasingly fashion (raw aesthetic)

sustainability.carbon_absorption: absorbs more CO2 per hectare than trees

recycled_pet.name: Recycled Polyester (rPET)
recycled_pet.process: post-consumer PET bottles or post-industrial polyester waste → melted/re-extruded into fiber

physics.note: properties virtually identical to virgin polyester after processing

tensile.note: equivalent to virgin PET in most applications

visual.surface: identical to virgin polyester
visual.note: no visual difference from virgin PET

sustainability.not_infinitely_recyclable: quality degrades after 2-3 cycles (mechanical); chemical recycling emerging

pla_polylactic_acid.name: PLA (Polylactic Acid)
pla_polylactic_acid.process: fermented plant starch (corn, sugarcane) polymerized into fiber

thermal.note: low melting point limits pressing and dyeing temperatures

chemical.note: does NOT biodegrade in landfill or ocean — requires specific conditions

visual.surface: similar to polyester, slight natural hand
visual.lightBehavior: matte to slight sheen

sustainability.debate: competes with food crops for corn feedstock

aramid.name: Aramid (Kevlar / Nomex)

thermal.note: does not melt — chars at extreme temperature

tensile.note: Kevlar is 5x stronger than steel by weight

physics.uv_resistance: poor — Kevlar degrades significantly in UV (must be covered)

visual.surface: Kevlar: golden yellow; Nomex: off-white to tan
visual.note: not used for fashion aesthetics — functional fabrics

production.primary_use: protective wear, military, firefighting, motorsport, cut-resistant gloves

aramid.fashion_application: emerging in avant-garde/technical fashion, used in reinforcement panels, protective fashion

uhmwpe.name: UHMWPE (Dyneema / Spectra)

morphology.note: floats on water — lightest high-performance fiber

thermal.note: low melting point is key limitation

tensile.note: strongest fiber commercially available — 15x stronger than steel by weight

visual.surface: white, smooth, slippery

production.primary_use: cut-resistant gloves, yacht sails, body armor, ropes

uhmwpe.fashion_application: Dyneema Composite Fabric used in ultra-lightweight technical outerwear, hiking gear

elastolefin.name: Elastolefin (XLA / Dow XLA)

thermal.note: heat-resistant up to 220C — can be dyed and finished at temperatures that destroy spandex

physics.chemical_resistance: resistant to chlorine, UV, high temperatures — solves all spandex weaknesses

visual.surface: similar appearance to spandex in blends

production.note: positioned as spandex replacement for harsh conditions

elastolefin.key_advantage: withstands chlorine, UV, and high heat that degrade spandex — ideal for swimwear and outdoor

pinatex.name: Piñatex (Pineapple Leaf Fiber)
pinatex.source: pineapple leaf fiber (PALF) — waste product from pineapple harvest

tensile.note: moderate strength, non-woven construction

moisture.note: breathable but not water-resistant without coating

visual.surface: leather-like texture, natural fiber visible at close range
visual.lightBehavior: matte, natural

production.primary_use: leather alternative for bags, shoes, accessories, fashion panels

seacell.name: SeaCell
seacell.process: lyocell-type process with seaweed (Ascophyllum nodosum) embedded in fiber

tensile.note: similar to lyocell

visual.surface: smooth, soft, subtle sheen similar to lyocell

production.note: claimed mineral/vitamin transfer to skin — scientifically debated

plain_weave.name: Plain Weave
plain_weave.method: 1/1 interlacing — warp and weft alternate over-under
plain_weave.visual_for_ai: flat uniform surface showing yarn interlacing pattern clearly, matte unless mercerized

poplin.name: Poplin / Broadcloth
poplin.method: Plain weave with higher warp than weft density, fine smooth finish
poplin.visual_for_ai: smooth crisp surface with slight sheen from tight weave, very fine texture, no visible yarn structure

oxford_cloth.name: Oxford Cloth
oxford_cloth.method: Basket weave variant — two weft yarns pass over and under two warp yarns
oxford_cloth.visual_for_ai: visible basket-weave texture with slightly rougher hand than poplin, casual appearance, soft matte finish

twill_weave.name: Twill Weave
twill_weave.method: Stepped interlacing creating diagonal lines — 2/1, 2/2, 3/1 patterns
twill_weave.visual_for_ai: visible diagonal lines on surface, texture along twill direction, semi-matte

herringbone.name: Herringbone
herringbone.method: Broken twill — twill direction reverses at regular intervals creating V-shaped zigzag pattern
herringbone.visual_for_ai: distinctive V-shaped zigzag pattern from alternating twill direction, creates depth and visual interest, classic menswear pattern

houndstooth.name: Houndstooth
houndstooth.method: Twill weave with alternating groups of 4 dark and 4 light yarns in both warp and weft
houndstooth.visual_for_ai: broken check pattern with distinctive jagged tooth-like shapes, classic black/white or two-tone, strong geometric repeat

denim.name: Denim
denim.method: 3/1 twill with indigo-dyed warp and natural (white) weft
denim.visual_for_ai: blue face with visible twill diagonal, white back showing weft yarns, fading patterns at wear points, whiskering at creases
denim.aging_note: develops unique patina with wear — fading, whiskering, honeycombs are valued

gabardine.name: Gabardine
gabardine.method: Steep twill (63-degree angle), very tight weave, smooth face, concealed back
gabardine.visual_for_ai: smooth tight surface with fine diagonal ridges, crisp and structured, slight sheen from tight weave
gabardine.note: water-resistant due to tight weave — original Burberry trench fabric

chambray.name: Chambray
chambray.method: Plain weave with colored warp and white weft — creates heathered appearance
chambray.visual_for_ai: soft heathered color effect from mixed warp/weft, lighter and more refined than denim, subtle color variation

satin_weave.name: Satin Weave
satin_weave.method: Long floats (5-7+ picks between interlacing), smooth lustrous face, dull back
satin_weave.visual_for_ai: extremely smooth face with high luster, directional sheen that shifts with angle, luxurious appearance, distinct face and back
satin_weave.care_note: delicate — long floats susceptible to snagging

sateen.name: Sateen
sateen.method: Satin weave using staple fiber yarns (typically cotton) instead of filament — softer sheen than true satin
sateen.visual_for_ai: smooth surface with soft luster (less sharp than satin), matte sheen rather than liquid shine, comfortable cotton hand with visual luxury

charmeuse.name: Charmeuse
charmeuse.method: Satin weave (typically silk or polyester), lightweight with extreme luster
charmeuse.visual_for_ai: liquid sheen on face, matte back, fluid drape that catches every light source, fabric appears to flow like water

taffeta.name: Taffeta
taffeta.method: Plain weave with fine, tightly woven yarns creating crisp body
taffeta.visual_for_ai: crisp smooth surface with distinctive rustling quality (frou-frou), subtle color shifting in shot/changeable taffeta, structured body

voile.name: Voile
voile.method: Plain weave with highly twisted fine yarns, open construction
voile.visual_for_ai: sheer lightweight fabric, semi-transparent, visible skin/layers beneath, soft ethereal quality, delicate movement

organza.name: Organza
organza.method: Plain weave with tightly twisted filament yarns, very stiff sheer
organza.visual_for_ai: crisp transparent fabric that holds shape, visible structure despite sheerness, creates volume through layering without weight

chiffon.name: Chiffon
chiffon.method: Plain weave with alternating S and Z twist yarns creating slight puckering
chiffon.visual_for_ai: sheer floaty fabric with slight texture from twist alternation, extremely lightweight, moves with slightest air current, ethereal quality

crepe.name: Crepe
crepe.method: High-twist yarns (2000-4000 TPM) or crepe weave creating pebbled/grainy surface texture
crepe.visual_for_ai: distinctly textured pebbled surface with matte finish, grainy appearance, no smooth sheen, beautiful drape despite texture

georgette.name: Georgette
georgette.method: Crepe variant — alternating S and Z twist crepe yarns in both warp and weft, open construction
georgette.visual_for_ai: sheer with distinctive grainy texture from crepe yarns, dull matte surface, slightly rough appearance, floaty with more body than chiffon

dupioni.name: Dupioni
dupioni.method: Plain weave using irregular double-cocoon silk yarns with natural slubs
dupioni.visual_for_ai: crisp fabric with distinctive irregular slub texture, subtle sheen from silk fibers, visible weft irregularities add character, structured body

flannel_cotton.name: Flannel (Cotton)
flannel_cotton.method: Plain or twill weave, brushed on one or both sides for nap
flannel_cotton.visual_for_ai: soft fuzzy surface from brushing, matte finish, warm cozy appearance, visible nap direction, softer edges to any pattern

flannel_wool.name: Flannel (Wool)
flannel_wool.method: Twill or plain weave, lightly fulled/milled then brushed, partially obscuring weave structure
flannel_wool.visual_for_ai: soft matte surface with slight fuzz from brushing, smooth drape with weight, sophisticated matte appearance, deep color saturation

canvas_duck.name: Canvas / Duck
canvas_duck.method: Plain weave, heavy weight, very tight construction
canvas_duck.visual_for_ai: heavy rugged fabric with visible weave structure, stiff and structured, does not drape, industrial aesthetic

jacquard.name: Jacquard
jacquard.method: Computer-controlled individual warp thread lifting creating complex woven-in patterns
jacquard.visual_for_ai: complex woven patterns integrated into fabric structure (not printed), reversible design, dimensional pattern, often contrasting face/back

dobby.name: Dobby
dobby.method: Small geometric patterns woven using dobby loom attachment — simpler than jacquard
dobby.visual_for_ai: small geometric textured patterns woven into surface (dots, small diamonds, waffle textures), subtle dimensional interest

brocade.name: Brocade
brocade.method: Jacquard weave with supplementary weft creating raised decorative pattern — often with metallic threads
brocade.visual_for_ai: luxurious raised pattern against contrasting ground, often with gold/silver metallic threads, rich dimensional surface, formal and ornate

damask.name: Damask
damask.method: Jacquard weave using satin and sateen weave areas to create tone-on-tone pattern — single color, pattern from weave structure
damask.visual_for_ai: subtle tone-on-tone pattern visible through interplay of matte and sheen areas, elegant restraint, pattern appears/disappears with light angle

moleskin.name: Moleskin
moleskin.method: Dense cotton satin weave, heavily brushed and sheared to create suede-like nap
moleskin.visual_for_ai: dense velvety surface resembling suede, matte finish, smooth short nap, warm and tactile, substantial weight
moleskin.note: not related to moles — named for the soft feel

drill.name: Drill
drill.method: Strong 2/1 or 3/1 twill weave, heavier than standard twill, very durable
drill.visual_for_ai: rugged twill with prominent diagonal, heavier and more structured than chino twill, workwear aesthetic

ripstop.name: Ripstop
ripstop.method: Plain or twill weave with reinforcing yarns at regular intervals (5-8mm grid) preventing tear propagation
ripstop.visual_for_ai: visible grid pattern from thicker reinforcing yarns, lightweight with distinct crosshatch texture, technical/military aesthetic

tweed.name: Tweed
tweed.method: Coarse wool twill or plain weave with multicolored yarns creating rustic textured appearance
tweed.visual_for_ai: multicolored flecked texture from blended yarns, rough surface with character, heavy and warm, traditional British countryside aesthetic

variants.harris_tweed: hand-woven in Outer Hebrides, legally protected

melton.name: Melton
melton.method: Heavily fulled/felted wool twill — weave structure completely obscured by felting
melton.visual_for_ai: dense smooth surface with no visible weave (obscured by felting), matte, very heavy, windproof, knife-edge finish possible
melton.note: does not fray when cut — no edge finishing needed

doeskin.name: Doeskin
doeskin.method: Satin weave wool, heavily napped and finished to create ultra-smooth surface
doeskin.visual_for_ai: extremely smooth surface resembling fine suede, soft matte finish, luxurious touch, formal appearance

single_jersey_knit.name: Single Jersey Knit
single_jersey_knit.method: Single needle bed — one row of loops per course
single_jersey_knit.visual_for_ai: vertical V-shaped wales on face, horizontal bumps on back, clean smooth knit surface, subtle stitch texture, body-following drape

rib_knit.name: Rib Knit
rib_knit.method: Double needle bed — alternating knit/purl columns creating visible ribs (1x1, 2x1, 2x2)
rib_knit.recovery: excellent — snaps back to original width
rib_knit.visual_for_ai: visible vertical rib channels creating shadow lines, textured surface, true double-faced, stretches significantly across ribs

interlock_knit.name: Interlock Knit
interlock_knit.method: Double needle bed — two sets of 1x1 rib interlock (every other needle on each bed)
interlock_knit.visual_for_ai: smooth compact surface, identical face and back, heavier than jersey, stable and uniform

french_terry.name: French Terry
french_terry.method: Knit with smooth face and looped back (loops not brushed)
french_terry.visual_for_ai: smooth jersey face, visible soft terry loops on inside, midweight with casual feel

fleece.name: Fleece (Brushed Back)
fleece.method: Knit with smooth face and brushed back (loops are brushed/napped into soft pile)
fleece.visual_for_ai: smooth face (slightly less crisp than french terry), soft fuzzy brushed back, bulky warm fabric, casual sporty

ponte_di_roma.name: Ponte di Roma
ponte_di_roma.method: Double knit interlock — two interlocked layers creating stable, structured knit
ponte_di_roma.visual_for_ai: smooth structured surface, heavier and more stable than jersey, holds shape like woven but with knit comfort

scuba_neoprene_knit.name: Scuba / Neoprene Knit
scuba_neoprene_knit.method: Double-faced spacer knit with foam-like structure between faces
scuba_neoprene_knit.visual_for_ai: thick structured fabric that holds shape dramatically, smooth both sides, does not drape, creates architectural volume, clean modern

pique.name: Pique
pique.method: Knit or woven with raised textured pattern (honeycomb, waffle, birdseye)
pique.visual_for_ai: raised textured surface with visible geometric micro-pattern (usually honeycomb), slightly dimensional, classic polo shirt fabric

waffle_knit.name: Waffle Knit / Thermal
waffle_knit.method: Knit with raised square grid creating three-dimensional honeycomb/waffle texture trapping air
waffle_knit.visual_for_ai: distinctive raised square grid pattern creating waffle-like texture, visible depth/shadow in each cell, casual textured look
waffle_knit.insulation_note: excellent warmth-to-weight from trapped air in cells

boucle_knit.name: Bouclé Knit
boucle_knit.method: Knit using loop/bouclé yarns creating textured, nubby surface
boucle_knit.visual_for_ai: highly textured surface with small loops and curls, nubby irregular appearance, Chanel-style texture, rich dimensional surface
boucle_knit.note: iconic Chanel bouclé tweed — typically woven but knit versions exist

spacer_knit.name: Spacer Knit / 3D Knit
spacer_knit.method: Two separate knit faces connected by spacer yarns creating air gap — 3D structure
spacer_knit.visual_for_ai: thick cushioned fabric with two distinct faces and air layer between, modern technical aesthetic, structured without stiffness
spacer_knit.insulation_note: excellent breathable insulation from air layer

tricot.name: Tricot (Warp Knit)
tricot.method: Warp knit — yarns loop in zigzag pattern along fabric length (vs. weft knit across width)
tricot.visual_for_ai: very smooth fine surface, slight diagonal line visible on back, thin and fluid, often with subtle sheen
tricot.production_note: extremely fast production — 10x faster than weft knitting

raschel_knit.name: Raschel Knit
raschel_knit.method: Warp knit with latch needles — can produce open-work, lace-like, and net structures
raschel_knit.visual_for_ai: varies widely — from lace-like open patterns to dense pile fabrics, versatile appearance

double_cloth.name: Double Cloth
double_cloth.method: Two separate woven layers connected at specific points — creates reversible fabric or structured heavy cloth
double_cloth.visual_for_ai: thick substantial fabric that can be two different colors/patterns on each face, reversible, architectural weight

velvet.name: Velvet
velvet.method: Double cloth woven face-to-face then cut apart creating dense short pile on face
velvet.visual_for_ai: dense short pile creates rich dimensional surface, dramatic light-dark variation as pile direction changes, luxurious depth of color, crushed areas show lighter tone

construction_notes.sewing: requires walking foot — pile layers shift
construction_notes.pressing: NEVER press face down — destroys pile. Use needle board or steam only
construction_notes.interlining: cannot use fusible — pile damage. Must use sew-in interlining

corduroy.name: Corduroy
corduroy.method: Weft pile weave — extra weft floats are cut creating parallel lengthwise ridges (wales)
corduroy.visual_for_ai: parallel vertical ridges (wales) creating strong directional texture, pile catches light differently than base creating depth, casual warm aesthetic

terry_cloth.name: Terry Cloth (Loop Pile)
terry_cloth.method: Warp pile loops on one or both sides creating absorbent surface
terry_cloth.visual_for_ai: visible loop pile texture on surface, thick and absorbent appearance, casual/athletic aesthetic

lace.name: Lace
lace.method: Open-work fabric created by interlacing, looping, or knotting yarns in patterns
lace.visual_for_ai: open-work pattern with visible design motifs against transparent ground, delicate intricate appearance, shows skin through openwork areas

types.guipure: heavier, connected motifs, no net ground

mesh.name: Mesh / Tulle
mesh.method: Open-construction knit or woven with regular grid of holes
mesh.visual_for_ai: regular grid of small openings, semi-transparent to transparent, lightweight, can be stiff (tulle) or soft (power mesh)

birdeye_knit.name: Bird's Eye Knit
birdeye_knit.method: Knit with small eyelet holes creating textured pattern and enhanced moisture wicking
birdeye_knit.visual_for_ai: small regular eyelet holes creating subtle textured pattern, athletic/technical appearance, visible ventilation

finishingProcesses._description: Textile finishing processes — mechanical and chemical treatments applied after fabric formation. Critical for the ConstraintEngine to validate finishing compatibility with fiber types.

mercerization.process: Immersion in 18-25% NaOH under tension, then neutralization
constraints: requires tension — slack mercerization gives different results

sanforizing.process: Mechanical compressive shrinkage — fabric pressed between rubber belt and heated cylinder
constraints: must be done before garment construction

calendering.process: Fabric passed through heated steel rollers under pressure
constraints: effect temporary on untreated cotton — washes out without resin

singeing.process: Fabric passed over gas flame or heated plate to burn off protruding fiber ends
constraints: NOT for synthetics (melting) or pile fabrics

brushing_napping.process: Fabric surface raised by rotating wire-covered rollers
constraints: creates nap direction — unidirectional cutting required

sueding.process: Fabric passed over sandpaper-covered rollers for fine peach-skin texture

shearing.process: Cutting surface fibers to uniform height with rotating cylinder blades

beetling.process: Repeated hammering of linen fabric with wooden fallers (20-40 hours)

decatising.process: Steam treatment of wool under controlled pressure

variants.full_decatising: pressurized steam, permanent set, higher luster

fulling_milling.process: Controlled felting of wool through moisture, heat, and mechanical action
constraints: wool only — other fibers don't felt
must control pH and temperature precisely

bio_polish.process: Cellulase enzyme treatment under controlled conditions — more aggressive than enzyme wash

resin_treatment.process: DMDHEU or similar crosslinking agent applied to cellulose

softener_treatment.process: Cationic, anionic, or silicone softeners applied

variants.silicone: best softness, most durable to washing

anti_pilling.process: Enzyme treatment or singeing to remove surface fibers; or resin coating

flame_retardant.process: Proban (phosphorus-based) or other FR chemicals applied
constraints: inherently FR fibers (wool, aramid, modacrylic) preferred over treated

water_repellent_DWR.process: Fluorocarbon (C6/C8) or silicone-based or wax-based coating
water_repellent_DWR.sustainability_note: C8 fluorocarbons banned; C6 under scrutiny; PFC-free alternatives emerging

waterproof_membrane.process: Laminated microporous membrane (PTFE, PU) or coating

antimicrobial.process: Silver ion, zinc pyrithione, or chitosan treatment

UV_protection.process: UV absorbers (benzotriazole) or TiO2/ZnO particles applied to fabric
constraints: tight-weave fabrics inherently have good UPF

stain_release.process: Hydrophilic polymer coating that allows water to penetrate and lift stains

heat_setting.process: Synthetic fabrics exposed to heat above Tg to stabilize dimensions
constraints: temperature must be precisely controlled per fiber type

dyeingSystems._description: Dye chemistry and print methods — critical for the ConstraintEngine to validate color achievability per fiber type

reactive.mechanism: forms covalent bond with cellulose hydroxyl groups
reactive.environmental: high salt and alkali usage, high water consumption

disperse.mechanism: dye migrates into fiber structure at high temp (thermosol) or with carrier
disperse.environmental: carrier chemicals toxic; high-temp method preferred

acid.mechanism: ionic bond between dye anion and fiber cation (amino groups)

vat.mechanism: water-insoluble dye reduced to soluble form, absorbed, then re-oxidized in fiber
vat.note: indigo for denim is a vat dye

direct.mechanism: dye has affinity for cellulose through van der Waals forces (no chemical bond)
direct.note: cheapest dye class for cellulosics — used where fastness not critical

sulfur.mechanism: macromolecular dyes formed inside fiber through sulfur reaction
sulfur.color_range: limited — primarily blacks, browns, navys, olives
sulfur.note: most economical dark shades for cotton — used in denim (sulfur black bottoms)

chrome_mordant.mechanism: metal salt (potassium dichromate) complexes with dye on fiber
chrome_mordant.environmental: chromium VI is highly toxic — being phased out globally
chrome_mordant.note: being replaced by reactive dyes for wool

natural.mechanism: plant/insect/mineral colorants with mordant fixation (alum, iron, tannin)
natural.note: growing niche market — sustainability appeal but limited scalability

pigment.mechanism: pigment particles bound to fabric surface with binder/resin — NOT a true dyeing process
pigment.note: most widely used for print (not piece dyeing) — can be applied to any fiber type

fiber_dyeing.disadvantages: must commit to color before construction

yarn_dyeing.typical_use: denim (indigo warp), plaid shirts, striped knits

garment_dyeing.advantages: latest possible color commitment, unique wash effects
garment_dyeing.disadvantages: uneven uptake at seams/heavy areas, limited to simple constructions
garment_dyeing.typical_use: casual wear, vintage/washed look, T-shirts, chinos

screen_printing.method: Ink forced through mesh screen stencil onto fabric

rotary_screen.method: Engraved cylindrical screens in continuous production line

digital_inkjet.method: Computer-controlled inkjet heads spray dye directly onto fabric — no screens needed

sublimation.method: Disperse dye printed on transfer paper, then heat-transferred to fabric at 200C
sublimation.note: dye becomes part of fiber — excellent wash fastness, no hand feel change

block_printing.method: Hand-carved wooden blocks pressed onto fabric

discharge_printing.method: Printed chemical removes/destroys dye from pre-dyed fabric
discharge_printing.effect: white or colored pattern on dark ground
discharge_printing.note: used for white prints on dark backgrounds — more vibrant than resist printing

resist_printing.method: Wax, paste, or mechanical resist prevents dye absorption in patterned areas

burn_out_devore.method: Chemical (acid) destroys cellulosic component of blend fabric, leaving pattern of sheer/opaque
burn_out_devore.note: requires specific fiber blend — acid destroys cellulose, leaves protein/synthetic intact

sustainabilityMetrics._description: Life Cycle Assessment data per fiber — enables ConstraintEngine to calculate environmental impact scores per design decision

garment_lifecycle_impact.cost_per_wear_concept: higher quality = lower impact per wear

Oeko_Tex_100.note: tests finished product, not production process

Cradle_to_Cradle.covers: circular design, material health, social fairness

FSC_PEFC.covers: forest management for wood-pulp based fibers (viscose, lyocell, modal)

colorScience._description: Color measurement and matching parameters for the ConstraintEngine — ensures color consistency across design, sampling, and production

CIE_Lab.use: industry standard for color specification and difference calculation
CIE_Lab.note: device-independent — same color described identically regardless of measurement device

CIE_LCh.use: more intuitive than L*a*b* for designers — describes color as hue/saturation/lightness

RGB.description: Red, Green, Blue additive color model (0-255 each)
RGB.note: device-dependent — same RGB values look different on different screens

CMYK.description: Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key(black) subtractive model
CMYK.note: smaller gamut than RGB — some screen colors cannot be printed

Pantone.description: Proprietary color matching system with physical swatch references
Pantone.use: industry standard communication between designer, mill, and factory
Pantone.note: gold standard for fashion color communication

delta_E_thresholds._description: CIE Delta E (ΔE) = perceived color difference between two samples
delta_E_thresholds.formula: CIEDE2000 (recommended — accounts for human perception nonlinearity)

thresholds.2.0_to_3.5: noticeable at a glance — typical pass/fail boundary

metamerism.definition: Two samples that match under one light source but differ under another
metamerism.cause: different spectral reflectance curves that produce same tristimulus values under specific illuminant

critical_illuminants.LED: emerging standard — varies by LED type

metamerism.constraint_rule: color approval must be evaluated under minimum 2 illuminants to catch metamerism

sublimation.note: critical for polyester — disperse dye migration at high temp

patternEngineering._description: Pattern cutting constraints driven by fabric properties — feeds the ConstraintEngine for scratch mode silhouette validation

sheer_fabrics.note: narrower to reduce visibility through fabric — French seams add bulk

leather_suede.note: narrower — no folding/pressing, glued or topstitched

fur_sheepskin.note: minimal — hand-sewn or machine with specialized foot

straight_grain.use: default for most pattern pieces — maximum stability, minimum stretch

cross_grain.use: specific design effects, striped fabric manipulation

bias.properties: maximum stretch and drape in woven fabrics
bias.use: bias-cut dresses, binding tape, cowl necklines

nap_direction.rule: ALL pattern pieces must run same direction for: velvet, corduroy, brushed fabrics, pile fabrics, satin (shading difference visible)

stripes.horizontal_matching: side seams, center front/back must align

plaids.matching: horizontal AND vertical alignment at seams, center front, pockets

large_prints.matching: motif placement on body — centering, avoiding awkward positioning

marker_efficiency.definition: percentage of fabric utilized vs. wasted in cutting layout

ease_allowances_cm.stretch_fabric_reduction: reduce ease by stretch percentage × recovery factor

stitch_classification.note: 6 stitch classes (100-600) as defined in V2 stitchClasses

seam_classification.note: 8 seam classes: SS, LS, BS, FS, OS, EF as defined in V2

fiber_content.labeling: FTC Textile Fiber Products Identification Act (US), EU Regulation 1007/2011

trims._description: Trims and notions — constrained by fabric type, garment category, and construction method

metal.use: denim, workwear, leather goods, premium outerwear

invisible.use: formal dresses, skirts, tailored garments — hidden in seam

two_way_separating.use: jackets, coats — opens from both ends

polyester_resin.use: most commercial garments — can mimic natural materials

fusible_woven.use: collars, cuffs, plackets — structured areas on wovens
constraints: NOT for velvet, pile, heat-sensitive fabrics

fusible_nonwoven.use: most common — waistbands, facings, general reinforcement
constraints: can stiffen fabric — choose weight carefully

fusible_knit.use: knit garments needing structure without losing stretch

sew_in_woven.use: velvet, silk, heat-sensitive fabrics where fusible would damage

hair_canvas.use: luxury tailoring — blazers, suits, coats (full or half canvas construction)
hair_canvas.note: gold standard for structured tailoring — shapes to body over time

woven.note: does not narrow when stretched — premium quality

clear_elastic.note: prevents stretching during wear — sewn into seam

cotton.note: shrinks with fabric — compatible stretch behavior

polyester_core_cotton_wrap.use: most commercial sewing — best of both
polyester_core_cotton_wrap.note: strength of polyester with cotton compatibility

polyester.use: synthetic fabrics, stretch fabrics, high-strength applications
polyester.note: does not shrink — caution with fabrics that do shrink

defectTaxonomy._description: Common fabric and garment defects — enables quality validation layer in production workflow

oversized.ai_visual: fabric holds its own shape, creates architectural volume away from body

fitted.ai_visual: fabric follows body contours, smooth surface, shows body shape

structured.ai_visual: holds pressed lines, maintains shoulder/lapel shape, clean silhouette

draped_fluid.ai_visual: fabric falls in fluid cascading folds, follows gravity, soft volume

fabric_requirements.gsm: variable — even light fabrics if stiff
fabric_requirements.body: holds shape through stiffness not weight

voluminous_stiff.ai_visual: fabric creates volume through stiffness, stands away from body, crisp edges

bodycon.ai_visual: fabric hugs every contour, smooth unbroken surface, second-skin appearance

relaxed.ai_visual: effortless drape, slightly away from body, casual ease without structure

tailored.ai_visual: clean lines, defined shoulders, pressed creases, shaped waist — traditional menswear or womenswear tailoring

layered_sheer.ai_visual: multiple transparent layers creating depth and dimension, fabric visible through fabric, ethereal effect

architectural.ai_visual: geometric shapes, clean sharp edges, sculptural forms that defy natural drape

asymmetric_draped.ai_visual: uneven hemlines, cascading folds on one side, bias-cut panels that flow asymmetrically

cocoon_wrap.ai_visual: enveloping rounded silhouette that wraps around body, soft volume, egg-shaped form

peplum_flared.ai_visual: fitted through body then flares out at waist/hip, circular or godeted panels

fabric_requirements.fiber: polyester best, wool acceptable, cotton only with resin treatment

pleated.ai_visual: regular parallel folds creating accordion or knife-pleat pattern, dimensional texture
pleated.constraint: permanent pleating requires thermoplastic fiber (polyester) or chemical treatment (cellulosic)

blendRules._description: Common blend combinations and their effects — critical for the AI recommendation engine

cotton_polyester.effect: combines cotton comfort/breathability with polyester wrinkle resistance and durability
cotton_polyester.visual_impact: slightly more uniform than 100% cotton, less natural character
cotton_polyester.care_impact: easier care, lower shrinkage, faster drying
cotton_polyester.shrinkage_risk: differential shrinkage can cause puckering if not pre-treated

cotton_elastane.effect: adds stretch and recovery to cotton while maintaining breathability
cotton_elastane.visual_impact: smoother surface, follows body more closely
cotton_elastane.care_impact: avoid high heat — elastane degrades. No chlorine bleach
cotton_elastane.constraint: elastane degrades with chlorine, high heat, UV over time

cotton_modal.effect: improved softness and drape while maintaining cotton breathability
cotton_modal.care_impact: similar to cotton, slightly better shape retention

cotton_linen.effect: reduces linen stiffness and wrinkling while maintaining coolness
cotton_linen.visual_impact: smoother than pure linen, less dramatic wrinkle character

cotton_silk.effect: combines silk luster with cotton breathability, more durable than pure silk
cotton_silk.visual_impact: subtle sheen, less dramatic than pure silk

wool_polyester.effect: reduces cost, improves wrinkle resistance, maintains wool drape and warmth
wool_polyester.visual_impact: slightly less lofty than pure wool, more uniform surface

wool_cashmere.effect: adds cashmere softness to wool structure at lower cost than 100% cashmere
wool_cashmere.visual_impact: softer surface than pure wool, slightly more luxurious hand

wool_silk.effect: silk adds luster and drape to wool; wool adds warmth and body
wool_silk.visual_impact: subtle sheen on wool surface, improved drape

wool_nylon.effect: nylon dramatically improves abrasion resistance and durability
wool_nylon.note: standard blend for socks and heavy-wear suiting

wool_elastane.effect: adds comfort stretch and shape retention to wool suiting
wool_elastane.visual_impact: smoother fit, less bagging at knees/elbows

silk_cotton.effect: combines silk luster with cotton breathability, more durable than pure silk
silk_cotton.visual_impact: subtle sheen, less dramatic than pure silk

polyester_viscose.effect: viscose adds drape and softness, polyester adds durability and wrinkle resistance
polyester_viscose.visual_impact: fluid drape with good shape retention
polyester_viscose.note: common suiting blend — cost-effective alternative to wool

nylon_elastane.effect: high stretch, excellent recovery — primary swimwear and compression blend

nylon_polyester.effect: combines nylon's elasticity and softness with polyester's shape retention

linen_viscose.effect: viscose reduces linen stiffness, adds drape and softness

cashmere_silk.effect: silk adds luster and drape, cashmere adds warmth and softness
cashmere_silk.visual_impact: subtle sheen on cashmere surface, improved drape

alpaca_wool.effect: combines alpaca softness/warmth with wool resilience and elasticity
alpaca_wool.note: addresses alpaca's lack of elasticity (no crimp)

lyocell_cotton.effect: improved softness and moisture management vs 100% cotton

hemp_cotton.effect: hemp adds strength and antimicrobial properties, cotton adds softness
hemp_cotton.visual_impact: slightly softer than pure hemp, retains natural character

recycled_poly_cotton.effect: same as virgin poly/cotton blend, reduced environmental impact

polyester_elastane.effect: stretch with shape retention and wrinkle resistance
polyester_elastane.note: activewear standard — moisture-wicking polyester with elastane stretch

wool_alpaca_nylon.effect: alpaca softness + wool elasticity + nylon durability

viscose_nylon_elastane.visual_impact: ponte-like structured knit with good recovery

cotton_bamboo_viscose.note: check labeling requirements — must be labeled 'rayon made from bamboo'

wool_cotton.effect: wool warmth + cotton washability, more casual hand
wool_cotton.visual_impact: less lofty than pure wool, casual texture

tri_blend_cotton_poly_rayon.effect: cotton comfort + polyester durability + rayon softness and drape
tri_blend_cotton_poly_rayon.note: extremely popular for fashion t-shirts — the 'tri-blend tee'

tencel_merino.effect: lyocell moisture management + merino temperature regulation

silk_cashmere_blend.effect: silk adds luster, strength, and reduced pilling to cashmere
silk_cashmere_blend.visual_impact: subtle sheen, reduced pilling vs 100% cashmere

fabric_garment_compatibility.rule: Fabrics with <5% stretch cannot be used for bodycon silhouettes
Silk charmeuse and chiffon cannot be used for structured blazers — insufficient bending rigidity
Canvas and heavy denim (>350 GSM) cannot be used for draped/fluid silhouettes
Organza maintains volume through stiffness — suitable for A-line but not body-conscious
Single jersey curls at edges — designs with exposed edges require rib or interlock
Velvet, corduroy, and all napped fabrics MUST use unidirectional cutting layout — adds 10-15% fabric
Silk and slippery fabrics require walking foot mechanism — standard feed causes layer shifting
Knit fabrics REQUIRE over-edge (class 500) or cover-seam (class 600) stitching — lockstitch alone will crack
Stretch fabrics MUST use differential feed mechanism or seams will pucker
Lace and mesh require stabilizer layer during sewing
Permanent pleats require thermoplastic fiber (polyester) or resin treatment (cotton) — untreated natural fibers will not hold pleats
Scuba/neoprene knit does not drape — cannot be used for wrap, cowl, or fluid styles
Puff/gathered sleeves require fabric with body — fluid fabrics collapse flat
Removing lining from a blazer requires shell fabric with B > 0.08 gf·cm² to maintain shape
Bias-cut garments require 15-25% more fabric than straight-grain layouts
Plaid/stripe matching at seams requires 15-25% extra fabric
Sheer fabrics require French seams or Hong Kong finish — raw seam allowances visible through fabric
Leather/suede cannot be pinned — clips or tape only. Needle holes are permanent
Sequined/beaded fabrics require seam allowance clearing before sewing — remove embellishments from seam zone
Bonded/laminated fabrics cannot be eased — no steam shrinking possible. Patterns must fit without easing
Knit garments should be cut with pattern pieces in the same direction on the knit (all pieces same way on the bolt) to avoid shade variation
Fabrics >400 GSM may require industrial sewing equipment — domestic machines lack power and throat space
Cashmere, vicuña, and ultra-fine animal fibers require hand finishing — aggressive pressing damages fiber surface
Linen wrinkles are inherent — designing 'wrinkle-free linen' without resin treatment is physically impossible
Melton cloth does not fray — raw edges acceptable in design (cut-edge details)
Double cloth can be separated at edges for reversible detail — design opportunity unique to this construction
Spacer knit/3D knit provides built-in padding — no additional padding needed for structured shoulders or bra cups
Ripstop fabric maintains strength when torn — a tear will not propagate beyond the reinforcing grid

interlining_constraints.rule: Synthetic fabrics: fusible interlining temperature cannot exceed 130°C
Silk: standard fusible interlining will damage — use heat-resistant fusible or sew-in
Velvet: cannot use ANY fusible interlining — pile will be permanently crushed. Must use sew-in only
Interlining weight must not exceed shell fabric weight
Lightweight fabrics (<150 GSM): light interlining only or risk stiffness showing through
Collars, cuffs, plackets, waistbands, and button bands require interlining for structure
Fusible interlining requires factory testing with specific temperature control tape before production run
Knit garments requiring structure should use fusible knit interlining to maintain stretch
Hair canvas (bespoke tailoring) must be pad-stitched to lapel for proper roll — fusible cannot replicate this
Waterproof/laminated fabrics: fusible adhesive may not bond — test required

pressing_constraints.rule: Wool: press at 140-160°C with light steam and press cloth — direct iron causes shine
Polyester/Nylon/Acrylic: press at 110-150°C, NO steam or minimal — risk of melting/glazing
Silk: press at 110-120°C, minimal steam only — water spots permanently mark silk
Spandex/Elastane blends: press at 80-120°C, NO steam — heat degrades elastic fiber
Polypropylene: press at max 66°C — extremely heat sensitive, will melt
Velvet: NEVER press face down. Use needle board, velvet board, or steam hover only
Acetate: dissolves in acetone — keep away from nail polish remover, certain adhesives
Dark wool: always use press cloth — direct contact causes irreversible shine
Leather/suede: low heat, no steam, press from back side only with protective cloth
PLA fiber: max press temp 150°C — exceeding will melt fiber (lower Tm than polyester)
Aramid (Nomex/Kevlar): no pressing concerns — withstands extreme heat (decomposition >500°C)

construction_method_constraints.rule: Class 100 chain stitch: temporary basting only — cannot be used for permanent seams (unravels from one end)
Lockstitch (class 300): most versatile for wovens but insufficient stretch for knit seams
Lapped seams (LS) required for high-stress areas: jeans side seams, workwear
Flat seams (FS) preferred for underwear and children's wear — reduces bulk against skin
Needle size must match fabric weight: lightweight (<120 GSM) = 60-70; medium (120-250 GSM) = 80-90; heavy (250+ GSM) = 100-120
Thread type must match fabric: natural fabrics use cotton/poly-core-cotton thread; synthetics use polyester thread
Welded/bonded seams only possible with thermoplastic synthetic fabrics — cannot weld natural fibers
French seam required for sheer fabrics where seam allowance would be visible
Stitch density (SPI): lightweight 12-16; medium 10-12; heavy 8-10. Too tight = puckering, too loose = weak seam
Ballpoint/stretch needles required for knits — sharp needle cuts loops causing laddering
Microtex/sharp needles required for microfiber, silk, tightly woven fabrics — standard needle leaves visible holes
Leather needles (cutting point) required for leather/vinyl — round point cannot penetrate
Topstitching thread (Tex 90+) requires larger needle (100-110) — cannot use standard needle size
Ultrasonically welded seams: polyester and nylon only — requires thermoplastic fiber at >65% content
Tape-sealed seams required for waterproof garments — stitching alone creates needle holes that leak

closure_constraints.rule: Invisible zippers for formal/aesthetic-critical garments; metal for workwear/denim; coil for casual/sport
Shell/natural buttons for premium garments; plastic for casual/budget
Closures must not be placed in high-stress areas on thin fabrics without reinforcement (interfacing or stay)
Hook-and-eye closures for waistbands, bra closures, skirt plackets — not for high-stress openings
Snap fasteners: holding power limited — suitable for lightweight/children's garments only
Metal zippers on delicate fabrics risk snagging — coil or invisible preferred for silk, chiffon, satin
Button weight must be proportional to fabric weight — heavy buttons on lightweight fabric cause sagging
Magnetic closures: not suitable near pacemakers or electronic devices — medical/safety constraint

lining_constraints.rule: Lining weight must equal or be lighter than shell fabric
Lining fabric should be preshrunk or have matched shrinkage rate with shell
Lining fabric should be slippery (cupro, polyester lining, silk) to allow garment to slide over body
Tailored jackets, coats, and structured dresses should be lined for comfort and shape retention
Lined garments need ease allowance 1-2cm larger than unlined equivalent to accommodate lining bulk
Static-prone shells (polyester, nylon) benefit from anti-static lining (cupro > polyester for this)
Sheer garments: lining color affects visible shade — must account for color interaction
Stretch garments: lining must also stretch or be cut smaller to not restrict movement

care_compatibility.rule: Mixing fibers with different shrinkage rates risks puckering: cotton body + polyester trim is problematic without pre-shrinking
Viscose rayon loses 40-50% strength when wet — construction must account for this (avoid stress seams without reinforcement)
Wool without superwash treatment will felt with agitation + heat + moisture
Spandex/elastane degrades with chlorine bleach — care labels must prohibit
Acetate dissolves in acetone — dry cleaning solvents must be verified (no perchloroethylene alternatives containing ketones)
DWR (water repellent) finishes degrade with detergent — specialized wash or reapplication needed
Silk yellows irreversibly in sunlight — not suitable for sun-exposed garments without UV treatment
Nylon yellows with age and UV exposure — not suitable for white garments with long lifecycle
Garment dyeing only viable on simple constructions — multi-component garments with different fiber trims dye unevenly
Enzyme-washed cellulosics lose 5-10% strength — construction must start with adequate fabric weight margin

costing_constraints.rule: Silk ($15-80/m) incompatible with budget tier (<$30 retail) — margins impossible
Cashmere ($40-200/m) requires luxury or premium price tier
Custom fabric development MOQ typically 1000-3000 meters — below this, use stock/greige
Velvet and jacquard require specialized equipment — CMT rates 20-40% higher than basic construction
Digital printing has no MOQ — viable for sampling and short runs. Screen printing needs 50-500m minimum
Sustainability certifications (GOTS, GRS) add 10-30% to fabric cost — must be reflected in retail pricing
Fabric utilization below 75% makes the garment economically unviable at scale — simplify pattern or resize marker
Pattern matching (plaids, large repeats) adds 15-25% fabric cost — factor into BOM
Vicuña ($400-3000/m) requires ultra-luxury tier only — fewer than 10 brands globally can justify this
Natural dyes cost 3-10x more than synthetic dyes — limited to artisanal/luxury positioning

dyeing_constraints.rule: Polyester ONLY accepts disperse dyes — reactive, acid, and direct dyes will not bond
Protein fibers (wool, silk, cashmere) require acid dyes — reactive dyes also work but at higher cost
Blended fabrics may require dual-bath dyeing (one bath per fiber type) — increases cost and complexity
Cotton sublimation printing requires minimum 65% polyester content — pure cotton won't hold sublimation dyes
Garment dyeing uneven at heavy seam areas, buttons, zippers — simple constructions only
Color matching must be evaluated under minimum 2 illuminants (D65 + TL84) to detect metamerism
Deep/dark colors on polyester risk sublimation fastness — dye migrates in heat (pressing, tumble drying)
Natural dyes have limited color gamut — cannot achieve neon, pure black, or very bright saturated colors
UHMWPE (Dyneema) cannot be conventionally dyed — surface is too hydrophobic. Solution dyeing required
PLA fiber dyes at lower temperature than PET — standard disperse dye processes will damage PLA
Indigo (vat dye) does not penetrate fiber core — this is what enables denim fading/aging. Ring-dyeing effect is intentional
Digital printing requires pre-treatment of fabric — untreated fabric produces blurry/dull prints

sustainability_constraints.rule: GOTS certification requires minimum 70% organic fiber content — check blend ratios
Recycled polyester (rPET) cannot be infinitely recycled mechanically — quality degrades after 2-3 cycles
Bamboo viscose must be labeled 'rayon made from bamboo' per FTC — cannot claim natural/organic bamboo
PLA (Ingeo) requires industrial composting (58°C) — will NOT biodegrade in landfill, home compost, or ocean
All synthetic fabrics shed microplastics in washing — design consideration for environmental impact
Cashmere has severe environmental impact from goat overgrazing — Mongolian desertification link
Fluorocarbon DWR (C8) banned in EU; C6 under scrutiny — specify PFC-free alternatives for new designs
Viscose production uses carbon disulfide (CS2) — toxic to workers. Lyocell (NMMO solvent, closed-loop) preferred
Wood-pulp fibers (viscose, modal, lyocell) require FSC/PEFC certified sources to avoid deforestation
Chrome mordant dyes for wool contain hexavalent chromium (Cr VI) — carcinogenic, being phased out globally

finishing_constraints.rule: Mercerization only works on cellulosic fibers (cotton, linen) — NOT on protein or synthetic fibers
Heat setting must be done before dyeing on synthetics — otherwise dimensions unstable
Enzyme treatments (bio-polish, enzyme wash) only work on cellulosic fibers — cellulase cannot digest protein or synthetic
Resin treatment (wrinkle-free) reduces cotton strength by 20-40% — specify heavier base fabric to compensate
Singeing must be done before desizing/scouring — singed fiber ends interfere with subsequent chemical processes
Calendering effect on untreated cotton washes out — requires resin fixation for permanence
Softener treatment reduces water absorbency — avoid on performance/moisture-wicking garments
Anti-pilling treatment reduces fabric strength slightly — specify adequate base weight
Lyocell fibrillates when wet — must be treated with crosslinking agent or use for intentional peach-skin effect
Waterproof membrane lamination requires seam taping — untaped seams leak at needle holes regardless of membrane quality

200.name: Hand Stitches
200.use: luxury, couture, tailoring, pad stitching, pick stitching

300.name: Lockstitches
300.use: most common, versatile, all wovens, topstitching

400.name: Multi-thread Chain
400.use: elasticated seams, athletic wear, seaming + serging combined

500.name: Over-edge Chain (Serger/Overlock)
500.use: edge finishing, serging, knit seaming, seam + finish combined

600.name: Cover-seam Chain (Coverstitch)
600.use: knit binding, underwear, athletic hemming, decorative visible stitching

SS.name: Superimposed Seam
SS.use: standard seaming — side seams, shoulder seams, most construction

LS.name: Lapped Seam
LS.use: jeans, workwear, yokes — high durability, flat construction

FS.name: Flat Seam
FS.use: underwear, children's wear, activewear — flat against skin, no bulk

EF.name: Edge Finishing
EF.use: hems, serged edges, raw edge prevention, clean finishing

pear.description: Hips wider than bust by 5+ cm

KES_F_reference._description: Kawabata Evaluation System for Fabric — objective hand measurement. Used by ConstraintEngine for silhouette compatibility scoring.

FAST_system._description: Fabric Assurance by Simple Testing — faster/cheaper alternative to KES-F
